facts.title: Shoes off indoors
facts.fact: You remove your shoes before entering a Korean home, and often at traditional restaurants with floor seating. Because floors are heated, sitting on the floor is comfortable.
facts.korean_en: I take off my shoes in the house.
facts.note: **온돌** (ondol) is the traditional under-floor heating system.

facts.title: Age shapes everything
facts.fact: Age shapes language and relationships, so people often ask your age early on. In 2023 Korea officially switched to international age counting for legal use, retiring the older "Korean age."
facts.note: A more casual version is **몇 살이에요?** Use the polite form with people you have just met.

facts.title: Bowing as a greeting
facts.fact: A small bow of the head is the everyday way to greet, thank, and say goodbye in Korea. The deeper the bow, the more respect it shows, so you bow lower to elders and superiors.
facts.korean_en: I bow my head to greet an elder.
facts.note: A light nod works among peers; **숙이다** means to lower or bend (the head).

facts.title: Give and receive with two hands
facts.fact: Handing or receiving something with **both hands** shows respect, especially toward elders. You can also support your right forearm with your left hand as a polite alternative.
facts.korean_en: I receive the object with both hands.
facts.note: **두 손** literally means "two hands." Using one hand can seem careless with people older than you.

facts.title: Pouring drinks for elders
facts.fact: At the table you pour drinks for others rather than yourself, and you hold the bottle with **two hands** when serving an elder. It is polite to keep an eye on others' glasses and refill them.
facts.korean_en: I pour a drink for my grandfather. (humble)
facts.note: **드리다** is the humble form of "to give," used when serving someone older.

facts.title: Turn away to drink
facts.fact: When drinking alcohol in front of an elder, you turn your head slightly to the side and cover the glass with your hand. This small gesture is a sign of modesty and respect.
facts.korean_en: I turn my head to the side and drink.
facts.note: You usually wait for the eldest person to take the first sip before you drink.

facts.title: Family name comes first
facts.fact: Korean names put the **family name first**, followed by the given name, which is usually two syllables. The surname Kim alone is shared by roughly one in five Koreans.
facts.korean_en: My family name is Kim and my given name is Minjun.
facts.note: To ask an elder's name politely, use **성함이 어떻게 되세요?**

facts.title: Exchanging business cards
facts.fact: Business cards are exchanged with **both hands** at the start of a meeting. You take a moment to read the card and place it on the table rather than stuffing it away immediately.
facts.korean_en: We exchange business cards with both hands.
facts.note: Reading the card shows interest; writing on it in front of the giver is considered rude.

facts.title: Spoon for rice and soup
facts.fact: At the table the **spoon** is used for rice and soup, while chopsticks pick up side dishes. Unlike in some neighboring countries, you generally hold one utensil at a time.
facts.korean_en: I eat rice and soup with a spoon.
facts.note: Sticking chopsticks upright in rice resembles a funeral offering, so it is avoided.

facts.title: Leave the bowl on the table
facts.fact: Korean table manners say you should **not lift your rice or soup bowl** to your mouth. The bowl stays on the table and you bring the spoon up to you.
facts.korean_en: I do not lift the rice bowl.
facts.note: This is the opposite of Japanese custom, where lifting the rice bowl is normal.

facts.title: Let elders eat first
facts.fact: At a shared meal you wait for the **oldest person to pick up their spoon** before you start eating. Younger people also tend to match the elders' pace and finish around the same time.
facts.korean_en: The elder starts the meal first. (polite)
facts.note: Before eating, people often say **잘 먹겠습니다** ("I will eat well") to thank the host.

facts.title: The unlucky number four
facts.fact: The number **4** is considered unlucky because its Sino-Korean reading sounds like the word for death. Some buildings label the fourth floor "F" instead of 4 in elevators.
facts.korean_en: In Korea people dislike the number four.
facts.note: The Sino-Korean **사** (four) is a homophone of the word for death, so it feels ominous.

facts.title: Use titles, not first names
facts.fact: Adults rarely call each other by their first name alone. Instead you use a **title or role** such as teacher, manager, or older sibling, often after the surname.
facts.note: Even outside school, **선생님** is a respectful way to address an adult you don't know well.

facts.title: Calling older friends 'older sibling'
facts.fact: Younger people address slightly older friends with sibling words rather than names. A woman calls an older man **오빠** and an older woman **언니**; a man uses **형** and **누나**.
facts.korean_en: Older sister (to a woman), let's go together!
facts.note: These words extend beyond family to any close person who is a bit older.

facts.title: Receiving gifts with both hands
facts.fact: Gifts are offered and accepted with **both hands**, and a small polite refusal before accepting is common. People often open a gift later in private rather than in front of the giver.
facts.korean_en: I received the gift with both hands.
facts.note: On a first home visit, fruit or a small treat is a common gift.

facts.title: Queue and keep quiet in public
facts.fact: People line up in an orderly **queue** and keep their voices low in buses, trains, and quiet public spaces. Loud phone calls on transit are frowned upon.
facts.korean_en: I stand in line to take the bus.
facts.note: **줄을 서다** means "to line up." Keeping quiet on transit is part of public courtesy.

facts.title: Subway priority seats
facts.fact: Subway and bus cars have **priority seats** reserved for the elderly, pregnant women, and people with disabilities. These seats are usually left empty even when the car is crowded.
facts.korean_en: There are priority seats on the subway.

vocabulary.en: priority seat (for the elderly and weak)

facts.note: Younger riders often give up an ordinary seat to an elder too, not just the marked seats.

facts.title: Standing on the escalator
facts.fact: On escalators many people stand on one side to leave room for others, though transit authorities now ask riders to **stand still on both sides** for safety. Holding the handrail is the official guidance.
facts.korean_en: Please hold the handrail on the escalator.
facts.note: Safety campaigns discourage walking up moving escalators because of the fall risk.
